title: Cargo.toml in Rust
content:
### Cargo.toml

`Cargo.toml` is the manifest file for Rust projects. It contains metadata, dependencies, build configuration, and more.

---

### Key Sections

| Section | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `[package]` | Crate metadata (name, version, edition) |
| `[dependencies]` | Runtime dependencies |
| `[dev-dependencies]` | Dependencies for tests only |
| `[build-dependencies]` | Dependencies for build scripts |
| `[features]` | Feature flags for conditional compilation |
| `[profile.*]` | Compiler optimization settings |
| `[workspace]` | Multi-crate workspace configuration |

---

### Example Cargo.toml

```toml
[package]
name = "my_app"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
description = "A Rust application"
authors = ["Your Name <email@example.com>"]
license = "MIT"

[dependencies]
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
tokio = { version = "1.0", features = ["full"] }

[dev-dependencies]
criterion = "0.5"

[profile.release]
opt-level = 3  # max optimization
lto = true     # link-time optimization

[features]
default = ["std"]
std = []
```

> **Key Point:** Use `cargo add` to add dependencies from the command line. Specify `edition = "2024"` for the latest Rust edition features. Use `[profile.release]` to control release build optimizations.
codeExample:
[package]
name = "hello_world"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]
rand = "0.8"

# Adding dependencies:
# cargo add serde --features derive
# cargo add --dev criterion

quiz.question: Which section in Cargo.toml lists runtime dependencies?
